title: How does the Well-Being of Young Adults Compare to Their Parents’?
summary: This Treasury analysis examines how the economic and social well-being of young adults (ages 25-39) compares to their parents' generation, finding significant deterioration in labor force participation for young men, stagnation in male earnings (especially for those without college degrees), and rising relative prices for housing and childcare. However, it notes a recent surge in young household wealth between 2019-2022 and strong earnings growth for women with bachelor's degrees.
key_points: Young male labor force participation declined from nearly 95% in 1990 to below 90% currently, while women's participation rose to 78% during the pandemic recovery
Median real weekly earnings for young men without bachelor's degrees declined by 9.9% (some college) and 4.1% (high school only) from 1990-2023
Real median home prices nearly doubled from 1990 to present while wages grew only modestly
Real median wealth for ages 25-39 surged over 140% from 2019 to 2022, reaching historically high levels, following decades of stagnation
Young women with bachelor's degrees saw 15.1% earnings growth from 1990-2023, narrowing the gender wage gap
Housing, childcare, healthcare, and education costs all increased significantly faster than wages since 1990
why_it_matters: This analysis documents structural shifts in the labor market and living standards for younger Americans. The decline in male labor force participation and earnings stagnation for non-college-educated workers represents a persistent economic challenge with long-term fiscal and policy implications. However, this is policy research for archival purposes, not a market-moving catalyst.
macro_context: This is demographic and labor economics research, not a macro catalyst. It discusses long-term secular trends in labor force participation, earnings, wealth accumulation, and cost of living—topics relevant to fiscal policy and social welfare but not to near-term market catalysts or monetary policy decisions.
important_numbers: 95% to <90%: young male labor force participation 1990 to present
9.9%: decline in median weekly real earnings for young men with some college (1990-2023)
4.1%: decline for young men with high school only (1990-2023)
15.1%: earnings growth for young women with bachelor's degrees (1990-2023)
140%+: surge in real median wealth for ages 25-39 from 2019 to 2022
~90%: of U.S. counties where housing costs grew faster than incomes (2000-2020)
